concepts.title: Addition and Subtraction operation on a matrix
concepts.conceptBody:
Algebra of Matrices

(i) Addition of Matrices : Two matrices A and B can be added only if A and B are of same order. Sum is obtained by adding the corresponding elements of $\text{A}$ and $\text{B}$.

If $\text{A}={{\left[ {{\text{a}}_{\text{ij}}} \right]}_{\text{m}\,\,\,\,\text{n}}}\,\quad $ and $\text{B}={{\left[ {{\text{b}}_{\text{ij}}} \right]}_{\text{m}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\text{n}}}$,

then, $A+B={{\left[ {{a}_{ij}} \right]}_{m\,\,\,\,\,\,n}}+{{\left[ {{b}_{ij}} \right]}_{m\,\,\,\,\,\,n}}={{\left[ {{a}_{ij}}+{{b}_{ij}} \right]}_{m\,\,\,\,\,\,\,n}}$

For example: $\text{If }\quad \text{A}=\left[ \begin{array}{*{35}{l}} 1 & 5 & 2 \\ 3 & 7 & 6 \\ \end{array} \right]\quad \text{ and }\quad \text{B}=\left[ \begin{array}{*{35}{l}} 4 & 9 & 8 \\ 3 & 6 & 1 \\ \end{array} \right]$

then, since order of A and B is same, both are $2\quad 3$ matrices.

Therefore, we can add A and B.

$A+B=\left[ \begin{array}{*{35}{l}} 1 & 5 & 2 \\ 3 & 7 & 6 \\ \end{array} \right]+\left[ \begin{array}{*{35}{l}} 4 & 9 & 8 \\ 3 & 6 & 1 \\ \end{array} \right]$$=\left[ \begin{matrix} 1+4 & 5+7 & 2+8 \\ 3+3 & 7+6 & 6+1 \\ \end{matrix} \right]=\left[ \begin{matrix} 5 & 14 & 10 \\ 6 & 13 & 7 \\ \end{matrix} \right]$

Difference of matrices A and B of same order is obtained by adding A and - B. - B is obtained by changing the sign of each element of $\text{B}$.

A - B can also be obtained by subtracting from the elements of A the corresponding elements of B.A and B must be of same order.

For A and B of last example,

$A-B=\left[ \begin{array}{*{35}{l}} 1 & 5 & 2 \\ 3 & 7 & 6 \\ \end{array} \right]-\left[ \begin{array}{*{35}{l}} 4 & 9 & 8 \\ 3 & 6 & 1 \\ \end{array} \right]$

$=\left[ \begin{matrix} 1-4 & 5-9 & 2-8 \\ 3-3 & 7-6 & 6-1 \\ \end{matrix} \right]=\left[ \begin{matrix} -3 & -4 & -6 \\ 0 & 1 & 5 \\ \end{matrix} \right]$

Properties of Matrix Addition:

Property I: Matrix addition is commutative i.e. if $\text{A}$ and $\text{B}$ be any two $\text{m}\quad \text{n}$ matrices, then $\text{A}+\text{B}=\text{B}+\text{A}$.

Property II: Matrix addition is associative i.e. if $\text{A},\text{B}$ and $\text{C}$ be three $\text{m}\quad \text{n}$ matrices, then

$\text{A}+(\text{B}+\text{C})=(\text{A}+\text{B})+\text{C}$

Property III: Cancellation laws hold good for addition of matrices

i.e. if $\text{A},\text{B},\text{C}$, be any three $\text{m}\quad \text{n}$ matrices, then

(i) $\text{A}+\text{B}=\text{A}+\text{C}\Rightarrow \text{B}=\text{C}$ (left cancellation law)

(ii) $\text{B}+\text{A}=\text{C}+\text{A}\Rightarrow \text{B}=\text{C}$ (right cancellation law)

(ii) Negative of a Matrix: If A be a given Matrix then - $\text{A}$ is called the negative of matrix $\text{A}$ and all its elements are the corresponding elements of A multiplied by -1

Thus if $A=\left[ \begin{matrix} 2 & 3 & -1 \\ 6 & -4 & 2 \\ \end{matrix} \right]$

then $-A=\left[ \begin{matrix} -2 & -3 & 1 \\ -6 & 4 & -2 \\ \end{matrix} \right]$

(iii) Scalar Multiple of a Matrix: If A be a given matrix and$\text{k}$ is any scalar number real or complex. [We call it scalar $\text{k}$ to distinguish it from matrix $[\text{k}]$ which is 1 matrix] then by matrix $\text{kA}=\text{Ak}$ is meant the matrix all of whose elements are $\text{k}$ times of the corresponding elements of $\text{A}$.

If $\text{A}=\left[ \begin{array}{*{35}{l}} 2 & 3 & 1 \\ 5 & 2 & 4 \\ \end{array} \right]$

then $3~\text{A}=\left[ \begin{array}{*{35}{l}} 3.2 & 3.3 & 3.1 \\ 3.5 & 3.2 & 3.4 \\ \end{array} \right]$

or $3~\text{A}=\left[ \begin{array}{*{35}{l}} 6 & 9 & 3 \\ 15 & 6 & 12 \\ \end{array} \right]$

Similarly $-4~\text{A}=\left[ \begin{array}{*{35}{l}} -4.2 & -4.3 & -4.1 \\ -4.5 & -4.2 & -4.4 \\ \end{array} \right]$

$=\left[ \begin{matrix} -8 & -12 & -4 \\ -20 & -8 & -16 \\ \end{matrix} \right]$

concepts.title: Properties of transpose of a matrix
concepts.conceptBody:
Transpose of a matrix

Let $\text{A}$ be any matrix then the matrix obtained by interchanging its rows and columns is called the transpose of $\text{AO}$ and is denoted by ${{\text{A}}^{\prime }}$ or ${{\text{A}}^{\text{T}}}$. If $\text{A}$ is a $\text{m}\times \text{n}$ matrix then ${{\text{A}}^{\prime }}$ will be a $\text{n}$ matrix.

Example : $A=\left( \begin{array}{*{35}{l}}  1 & 2 & 3 \\  2 & 0 & 5 \\ \end{array} \right)\text{, then }{{A}^{\prime }}=\left[ \begin{array}{*{35}{l}} 1 & 2 \\ 2 & 0 \\ 3 & 5 \\ \end{array} \right]$ 

$\text{i}=1,2,\ldots ,\text{m}$

Note: If $A=\left| {{a}_{ij}} \right|$; $\text{j}=1,2,\ldots ,\text{n}$

$\text{j}=1,2,\ldots \text{n}$

Then A’ = [a ji ]; $\text{i}=1,2,\ldots ,\text{m}$

Property of transpose of Matrices:

Property I. $\mathbf{(A+B)}'\mathbf{=A}'\mathbf{+B}'$

Property II. If A is any matrix, then ${{\left( {{\mathbf{A}}^{\prime }} \right)}^{\prime }}=\mathbf{A}$

Property III. If $\text{k}$ is any number real or complex and $\text{A}$ be any matrix, then ${{(\text{kA})}^{\prime }}=\text{k}{{\text{A}}^{\prime }}$

Property IV. If A be a m $n$ matrix and B be an $p$ matrix, then ${{(\mathbf{AB})}^{\prime }}={{\mathbf{B}}^{\prime }}{{\mathbf{A}}^{\prime }}$.

concepts.title: Properties of complex conjugate of a matrix
concepts.conceptBody:
Complex Conjugate of a Matrix

If a matrix $\text{A}$ is having complex numbers as its elements, the matrix obtained from $\text{A}$ by replacing each element of $\text{A}$ by its conjugate $(\overline{a\pm ib}=a\mp ib)$ is called the conjugate of matrix $A$ and is denoted by $\bar{A}$.

For example:

$\text{ If }\quad A={{\left[ \begin{matrix} a+ib & x-iy & p-iq \\ \lambda -i\mu & \alpha +i\beta & \gamma +i\delta \\ r-is & \mu -iv & \theta +i\phi \\ \end{matrix} \right]}_{3\times 3}}$

$\text{ then }\quad \overline{\text{A}}={{\left[ \begin{matrix} \text{a}-\text{ib} & \text{x}+\text{iy} & \text{p}+\text{iq} \\ \lambda +\text{i}\mu & \alpha -\text{i}\beta & \gamma -\text{i}\delta \\ \text{r}+\text{is} & \mu +\text{iv} & \theta -\text{i}\phi \\ \end{matrix} \right]}_{3\times 3}}$

Note: If all elements of $\text{A}$ are real then $\overline{\text{A}}=\text{A}$.

Properties of Complex Conjugate of a Matrix

(i) $({\over{\text{A}}})=\text{A}$, i.e, conjugate of the conjugate of a matrix is the matrix itself.

(ii) $(\overline{\text{A}+\text{B}})=\overline{\text{A}}+\overline{\text{B}}$, i.e., the conjugate of the sum of the two matrices is the sum of their conjugates.

(iii) $(\overline{\text{kA}})=\text{k}\overline{\text{A}}$, where $\text{k}$ is any number.

(iv) $(\overline{\text{AB}})=\overline{\text{A}}\cdot \overline{\text{B}}$, where $\text{A}$ and $\text{B}$ being conformable to multiplication.

concepts.title: Properties of complex transpose of a matrix
concepts.conceptBody:
Conjugate Transpose of a Matrix

The conjugate of the transpose of a matrix $A$ is called the conjugate transpose of $A$ and is denoted by ${{A}^{\theta }}$. Thus, ${{A}^{\theta }}=$ Conjugate of ${{A}^{\prime }}=\left( \overline{{{A}^{\prime }}} \right)$

$\text{ If }\quad \text{A}=\left[ \begin{matrix} 2+4\text{i} & 3 & 5-9\text{i} \\ 4 & \alpha +\text{i}\beta & 3\text{i} \\ 2 & -5 & 4-\text{i} \\ \end{matrix} \right]$

$\text{ then }\quad {{A}^{-}}=\left( \overline{{{\text{A}}^{\prime }}} \right)=\left[ \begin{matrix} 2-4\text{i} & 4 & 2 \\ 3 & \alpha -\text{i}\beta & -5 \\ \mathbf{5}+9\text{i} & -3\text{i} & 4+\text{i} \\ \end{matrix} \right]$

Properties of Transposed Conjugate Matrix

(i) For any matrix$\text{A},{{(\overline{\text{A}})}^{\prime }}=\left( {{\overline{\text{A}}}^{\prime }} \right)$, i.e., the transposed conjugate of a matrix is equal to the conjugate of its transpose.

(ii) For any matrix$\text{A},{{\left( {{\text{A}}^{\theta }} \right)}^{\theta }}=\text{A}$

(iii) If$A$ and $B$ are two matrices conformable to addition, then ${{(A+B)}^{\theta }}={{A}^{\theta }}+{{B}^{\theta }}$

(iv) For a matrix $\text{A},{{(\text{kA})}^{\theta}}=\text{k}{{\text{A}}^{\theta }}$, where $\text{k}$ is a scalar.

(v) If $A$ and $B$ are two matrices conformable to the product A B, then ${{(AB)}^{\theta }}={{B}^{\theta }}{{A}^{\theta }}$
